display_name: Output queue drops indicate congestion, not interface errors — different root cause than input drops
context: show interface output showing drops in the output queue or total output drops counter
observation: Non-zero output drops or output queue drops on an interface
naive_interpretation: The interface has a hardware problem or is failing — similar to input errors
correct_interpretation: Output drops almost always indicate traffic congestion — more traffic is being sent to the interface than its line rate can handle. This is a traffic engineering or QoS problem, not a physical layer problem. Input drops/errors (CRC, runts, giants) indicate physical layer issues. Output drops indicate the egress queue is full because the aggregate ingress traffic destined for this interface exceeds its bandwidth.
why: Output drops occur when the egress ASIC queue for an interface overflows — this is a normal backpressure mechanism
The distinction maps to completely different troubleshooting paths: input errors -> check cables/optics/PHY, output drops -> check traffic patterns/QoS/link utilization
Output drops on a 1G interface receiving aggregate traffic from multiple 10G interfaces is expected behavior under load, not a defect

confirmation_commands.purpose: See per-queue drop counts to identify which traffic class is being dropped
Check if the interface is running at or near line rate — confirms congestion as the cause
Check QoS policy drops per class to identify the congested traffic class

traps: Treating output drops the same as input errors and investigating physical layer
Replacing optics or cables to fix output drops — this will never help
Not checking interface utilization to confirm the congestion hypothesis
Ignoring output drops because the interface shows no input errors — output drops are a real problem, just a different kind
